data.title: NBA Star Jaylen Brown Calls for Player Equity
data.description:
Jaylen Brown of the Boston Celtics, a four-time NBA All-Star, Finals MVP, entrepreneur and philanthropist says players should be allowed to build equity alongside owners, arguing the league's labor rules lag far behind the wealth created by the sport. He speaks with Brooke Sutherland in Boston.

BMW Quarterly Group Sales Fall on Weakness in China and U.S.
data.description:
By Dominic Chopping


BMW group vehicle sales fell in the fourth quarter as growth in Europe failed to offset a drop in the U.S. and hefty declines in China.

The German automaker, which houses its namesake brand as well as the Mini and Rolls-Royce marques and a motorcycle business, said sales fell 4.1% in the quarter to 667,981 vehicles.

However, for the year as a whole, sales edged 0.5% higher to 2.46 million vehicles.

"Our electrified vehicles were in particularly high demand," said Jochen Goller, member of the BMW board of management. "Europe reported especially strong growth, with battery-electric vehicles accounting for about a quarter of total sales, and battery-electric vehicles and plug-in hybrid electric-vehicles combined reaching a share of over 40% across the region."

The company said Friday that sales of BMW branded cars slipped 1.4% in 2025, with sales growth in Europe, the Americas and parts of Asia largely offsetting subdued demand in China.

Sales of Minis rose around 18% as its range of fully-electric vehicles in particular proved popular with customers globally. More than one in three Minis delivered in 2025 had a fully-electric drive train.

The Rolls-Royce brand delivered 5,664 units to customers, a 0.8% drop on year.

BMW will introduce several new models this year, such as the BMW X5, BMW 3 Series, and BMW 7 Series. The group plans to launch more than 40 new and revised vehicles with various drive options by 2027.


Write to Dominic Chopping at dominic.chopping@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

01-09-26 0549ET

Venezuela Is No Template for Taiwan, Eurasia's Wang Says
data.description:
While the US operation in Venezuela threatens to open up new flashpoints with China, Eurasia Group's China Director, Dan Wang, argues that it will not embolden Beijing to seize Taiwan through military force. data.title: German foreign ministry spokesperson: German foreign minister will go to Washington on Sunday, meet with Rubio on Monday
China Auto Sales Slumped in December Amid Slowing Demand
data.description:
By Jiahui Huang


China's auto sales fell last month as the country continued to grapple with slowing demand, a sign that business conditions could remain challenging in 2026 for the world's largest car market.

Retail sales of passenger cars dropped 14% to 2.26 million units in December from a year earlier, the China Passenger Car Association said Friday. Sales rose 1.6% from November.

For the full year, retail sales of passenger cars in China rose 3.8% to 23.7 million units.

The CPCA said December's weak performance was due to consumers taking a wait-and-see approach, as the government's trade-in subsidies were set to expire by the end of the year. Beijing late last month renewed the car trade-in program for 2026 as part of a broader effort to boost consumption.

China sold more electric vehicles than gasoline-powered cars last year. Retail sales of new-energy cars, a category that includes battery EVs and plug-in hybrids, accounted for 57% of passenger-vehicle sales in 2025, the association said.

Retail sales of NEVs rose 2.6% to 1.3 million units in December, bringing the annual figure to 12.8 million units, an 18% increase.

The growth momentum of car exports remained strong as automakers, faced with a saturated domestic market, continued their aggressive expansion abroad. Exports of passenger cars jumped 46% last month from a year earlier to 588,000 units, CPCA data showed.

Tesla delivered 97,171 cars made at its Shanghai plant to Chinese buyers, and exported 3,328 units.

The near-term outlook for the world's largest auto market is encouraging, however, as Chinese carmakers and dealers usually work hard to ensure a strong start to the year.

The CPCA said China's auto market is set for a cautious but stable start to 2026, thanks to early policy support, pent-up demand and a late Lunar New Year in mid-February. It noted a pattern of strong buying by migrant workers and first-time buyers before the Lunar New Year holidays, which will likely help boost January sales.

China's auto-market sales is likely to follow a U-shaped trajectory this year, with a strong growth phase early in the year, followed by a midyear slowdown, before a rebound toward the year-end, the CPCA said. Overall vehicle sales in the domestic market are expected to be broadly flat from 2025, while exports will likely maintain double-digit growth of more than 10%, it said.


Write to Jiahui Huang at jiahui.huang@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

01-09-26 0541ET

data.title: UK house sales were 8% higher in November than a year earlier — HMRC
data.description:
(Alliance News) - The estimated number of UK home sales in November last year was 8% higher than the same month in 2024, according to HM Revenue & Customs figures.

Around 100,350 homes changed hands in November 2025, which is 1% more than in October last year.

An estimated 732,310 home sales have taken place in the financial year to date, running from April to November 2025, compared with 746,220 a year earlier, according to the HMRC figures.

Richard Donnell, executive director at Zoopla, said: "The number of housing transactions was 8% higher in November 2025 as sales agreed in spring and early summer finally completed before the year-end.

"The November budget delayed buying decisions in the final quarter of the year but we expect a rebound in buyer demand in (the first quarter of) 2026 and there are early signs already feeding through since Boxing Day, which means a strong start to the year ahead. This will be welcome news for buyers and sellers."

Nick Leeming, chair of Jackson-Stops, said: "November's HMRC figures may look like a sudden bounce but they largely reflect the busy summer period feeding through into completed transactions.

"In reality, and particularly for higher-valued properties, the market spent much of November on pause as buyers and sellers waited for clarity ahead of the budget."

Jason Tebb, president of OnTheMarket, said: "Transaction numbers continue to hold up, illustrating the housing market's remarkable overall resilience in the face of wider economic and political concerns.

"With the budget done and dusted, uncertainty at least has been removed and those who put their moves on pause are returning to the market, encouraged by lower mortgage rates from some of the big lenders, with others expected to follow. As January progresses, well-priced homes continue to attract interest."

Lloyds Bank, Halifax and Barclays made reductions to some of the mortgage rates they are offering from Friday.

Nathan Emerson, chief executive officer at property professionals' body Propertymark, said: "As we move into 2026, improving affordability and clearer economic conditions should help sustain momentum, provided house prices remain steady, and lending conditions continue to ease."

Ian Futcher, a financial planner at wealth manager Quilter, said: "As we move into the new year, the convergence of greater post-budget clarity, lower interest rates and a degree of pent-up demand could support a modest improvement in activity.

"However, stretched affordability and still-elevated borrowing costs mean any resurgence is likely to be gradual rather than dramatic.

"Overall, the data points to a market that has stabilised but is waiting for a clearer signal before moving higher. A slow return to more buoyant conditions looks far more likely than any sudden spike in transactions as we head into 2026."

Sarah Coles, head of personal finance at Hargreaves Lansdown, said: "The start of 2026 could see some green shoots – thanks to wages rising ahead of house prices and mortgage rates trending downwards. Nobody is expecting this to be a massive year for property price growth, but there should be a January bump to bring a bit of much-needed enthusiasm back to the market."

By Vicky Shaw, Press Association Personal Finance Correspondent

Press Association: Finance

source: PA

Copyright 2026 Alliance News Ltd. All Rights Reserved.

Wingtech pursues international arbitration against Dutch state over Nexperia seizure
data.description:
AMSTERDAM, Jan 9 (Reuters) - China's Wingtech is seeking international arbitration in pursuit of up to $8 billion in damages following the Netherlands' seizure of chipmaker Nexperia, two sources familiar with the legal proceedings told Reuters.

The move, part of a broader fight over Nexperia which has upset supply chains for carmakers around the globe, could move the dispute from Dutch courts, where a key hearing is set for January 14.

Wingtech, which owns Nexperia, served formal notice to the Dutch foreign and economic affairs ministries on October 15, which opened a six-month period for the parties to settle before arbitration begins.

The Dutch state seized control of Nexperia on September 30, saying the move was needed to prevent the company's Chinese CEO from moving operations and intellectual property to China. The Dutch government suspended the move in November but its action prompted a breakdown between the company's European production and Chinese packaging and distribution arms.

Wingtech's case invokes Article 10 of the Netherlands-China investment treaty, which says that each country's investors should be treated on equal terms as domestic investors and compensated in the event of state interference.

The Dutch economic affairs ministry and Wingtech declined comment.

Under the treaty, arbitration would be heard at the International Centre for Settlement of Investment Disputes, which is part of the World Bank.

Steffen Hindelang, a professor of trade law at Sweden's Uppsala University who is not involved in the case, said the Dutch intervention at Nexperia was sufficient grounds for arbitration, though it was not possible to say what the result might be.

The Dutch state might argue that its intervention was legal and did not harm Nexperia's value.

However, any state action at a company "impacts the value of your investment and calls into question whether you can successfully divest", Hindelang said.

Nexperia reported $331 million in profit on $2.06 billion in sales in 2024.

(Reporting by Toby Sterling; Editing by Emelia Sithole-Matarise)

By Toby Sterling

Glencore Shares Jump on Talks With Rio Tinto to Create World's Largest Miner
data.description:
By Adam Whittaker


Shares in commodity giant Glencore climbed on talks with Rio Tinto for an all-share merger that would create the world's largest mining company.

In morning trade in London, Glencore shares rose 8.5% to 448 pence--their highest level in over a year. Despite the rise, shares still trade significantly below their peak in January 2023, and give the Switzerland-based commodity trading and mining group a market capitalization of roughly $70.43 billion.

As merger-and-acquisition activity across the mining sector heats up, Glencore has been viewed by some analysts as a potential takeover target.

Glencore's shares have been weighed down recently in part by its coal division and a weaker copper business relative to companies that focus entirely on mining the red metal.

The expectation is that Rio Tinto would buy Glencore, according to the companies. The deal would include a possible combination of some, or all of their businesses. The combined entity would have a market value of more than $200 billion. The companies said the talks were at a preliminary stage.

Beyond this, little is known on the structure of any deal.

"We would not expect Glencore to get taken out in a nil-premium merger," Jefferies analysts said in a note, referring to the structure of Anglo American's recent deal to buy Canada's Teck in which the bidder didn't offer a premium to combine with the target.

Assuming Glencore sells its coal business, the merged entity would likely trade at premium to the weighted average of the two companies, Jefferies analysts added.

Copper would be at the heart of the combined business. Rio Tinto wants Glencore's copper growth potential, and exposure to the metal could lift the combined entity's shares, according to analysts.

The industrial metal is a core component in wiring and will be central to global electrification efforts. A series of supply shocks at major mines have helped push copper to record highs this week.

New mines are expensive to build and mean merger and acquisitions are the quickest and easiest way of securing new supply. The clamor for copper has sparked deal talks between the major miners, and is the driving force behind the Anglo American-Teck Resources deal.

Still, a tie-up between Rio Tinto and Glencore isn't guaranteed to happen. The talks revisit a combination that was first discussed around a year ago but broke down. Regulatory hurdles and the future of Glencore's coal and trading units within a combined group could pose hurdles to reaching a deal.

There is also the possibility an interloper could emerge. "BHP could look to acquire Glencore with a rival bid," Berenberg's Richard Hatch and Jasper Mainwaring write.

BHP--currently the world's No. 1 miner by market value--didn't immediately respond to a request for comment. It previously bid for Anglo American but was rebuffed, and risks falling behind rivals who are snapping up copper assets.

Glencore has been marketing itself for a sale for some time and is seen a willing seller, RBC Capital Markets analyst Ben Davis wrote in a note to clients.


Write to Adam Whittaker at adam.whittaker@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

01-09-26 0530ET

Chinese AI Firm MiniMax Soars in Hong Kong Trading Debut
data.description:
MiniMax, one of China's largest generative AI startups, surged in its trading debut in Hong Kong after raising $619 million in its initial public offering. Co-founder and Chief Operating Officer, Yeyi Yun, says the company plans to use the proceeds for R&D for foundation models. She also discusses overseas expansion plans on Bloomberg's The China Show.
